37: kai gar to peri emou telos echei — for what is written about me has its fulfillment — Luke's unique passion-eve citation of Isaiah 53:12 (he was numbered with the transgressors) is the only place in the Synoptics where Jesus explicitly quotes a Servant Song as being about himself and being fulfilled in the impending events. Telos echei (has its end/completion) uses the teleological vocabulary of purpose reaching its goal; the Servant vocation that Jesus has lived throughout the Gospel is now reaching its culminating moment in the passion.
1: hē heortē tōn azymōn hē legomenē pascha — the Festival of Unleavened Bread called Passover — Luke identifies the two festivals by their relationship: heortē tōn azymōn (Feast of Unleavened Bread — azymos = unleavened, without yeast; the seven-day festival following Passover, Leviticus 23:6) and pascha (Passover — from Aramaic paskha, Hebrew pesaḥ = pass over, the Exodus-commemoration sacrifice). By the first century the two festivals were conflated in popular usage as "Passover week." Ēngizen (was drawing near — imperfect of engizō; Luke's approach verb) signals the imminence of the passion timeline.
2: ezētoun hoi archiereis kai hoi grammateis to pōs anelōsin auton — the chief priests and teachers of the law were looking for some way to put him to death — zēteō (seek, look for — the ongoing search verb; imperfect indicating continuous plotting) + to pōs (how — the indirect question articular infinitive = "the how of killing him") + anelōsin (destroy, put to death — aorist subjunctive of anaireō = take up/away; the killing verb used throughout Acts for executions). Ephobounto gar ton laon — for they feared the people — phobeomai (fear) + ton laon (the people — laos = the covenant people; the crowd's protection of Jesus is the political constraint on the authorities).
3: eisēlthen de Satanas eis Ioudan ton kaloumenon Iskariōtēn — Satan entered into Judas called Iscariot — Luke's explicit statement that Satan entered Judas is unique among the Synoptics (only John 13:27 has a parallel, during the Last Supper); Luke places it before the conspiracy. Eiserchomai eis (enter into) is the same construction used for demonic possession throughout Luke (Luke 8:30, 33; Luke 11:26); the use for Satan's entry into Judas places the betrayal in the category of demonic agency. Iskariōtēs (Iscariot — possibly from Hebrew ʾîš qerîyôt = man of Kerioth, a town in Judea; alternatively from Aramaic šeqarya = falseness). Ontos ek tou arithmou tōn dōdeka — being from the number of the Twelve — the insider status makes the betrayal worse.
4: apelthōn synelalésen tois archiereusin kai stratēgois — going away, he conferred with the chief priests and officers — synlaleō (speak with, confer with — from syn- + laleō = talk together; used at 9:30 for Moses and Elijah speaking with Jesus at the Transfiguration; now used for Judas's treacherous conference). Stratēgois (officers — from stratēgos = military commander, general; here the officers of the temple guard, the temple police; also Acts 4:1; Luke 5:24-26) are the temple security force under the chief priests. The collusion includes both religious leadership and their enforcement arm.
5: echarēsan kai synethento autō argyrion dounai — they were delighted and agreed to give him silver — chairō (rejoice, be delighted — the joy verb; the authorities' joy contrasts sharply with heaven's joy over one repenting sinner in Luke 15) in the aorist: the moment of relief at finding a solution. Syntithēmi (agree, covenant together — from syn- + tithēmi = put together; to make a joint arrangement) + argyrion dounai (to give silver — argyrion = silver coin, money; the betrayal price is not specified here, unlike Matthew 27:3's thirty pieces of silver).
6: kai exōmologēsen kai ezētei eukairian tou paradounai auton — he consented and was looking for an opportunity to betray him — exomologeō (consent, agree — from ex- + omologeō = confess, acknowledge; here in the sense of agreeing/committing to the arrangement). Ezētei eukairian (was looking for a favorable moment — imperfect, ongoing search; eukairos = good-time, favorable moment = the window of opportunity). Ater ochlou (without a crowd — ater is a rare preposition meaning "without, apart from"; the crowd's presence throughout the Jerusalem days protects Jesus from arrest; Judas's task is finding the uncrowded moment).
7: ēlthen de hē hēmera tōn azymōn, hē edei thyesthai to pascha — the day of Unleavened Bread came, on which the Passover lamb had to be sacrificed — edei thyesthai (it was necessary to sacrifice — the divine necessity dei governing the Passover sacrifice; the same necessity-verb that governs the passion, Luke 9:22; Luke 24:7). Thyō (sacrifice, slaughter — the technical sacrificial term; the Passover lamb is to pascha, the sacrifice itself). The necessity of the Passover sacrifice is placed in the same divine-necessity frame as Jesus's coming death, creating the theological resonance that Luke develops through the passion narrative.
8: apesteilen Petron kai Iōannēn — he sent Peter and John — the leadership pair of the early church (Acts 3:1; Luke 4:13; Luke 8:14) are commissioned together for the Passover preparation; this is Luke-distinctive (Mark and Matthew say "two disciples"). Poreutheutes hetoimasate hēmin to pascha hina phagōmen — go and prepare the Passover for us so that we may eat — hetoimazō (prepare — the Passover-preparation verb; the disciples fulfill the same role as the disciples sent ahead for the triumphal entry, Luke 19:29-30); the purpose clause hina phagōmen (so that we may eat) sets the goal of the entire preparation activity.
9: hoi de eipan autō, pou theleis hetoimasōmen — they said to him, where do you want us to prepare — the disciples' question is practical and presupposes Jesus's knowledge of a prepared location. The verb theleis (you want/will — from thelō) places the choice entirely with Jesus; the disciples are implementing his intention, not making independent arrangements. The implied background is that Jesus has already arranged the upper room in advance, and the sign (man with water jar) is a prearranged signal.
10: anthrōpos keramion hydatos bastazōn apantēsei hymin — a man carrying a jar of water will meet you — keramion (clay jar — from keramos = clay, pottery) + hydatos (of water — genitive) + bastazōn (carrying — the carrying verb). In antiquity, carrying water was typically women's work; a man carrying a water jar was unusual and therefore a recognizable identifying sign. Akolouthēsate autō eis tēn oikian — follow him into the house — the sequence (follow → enter → ask the owner) describes a prearranged contact system, suggesting Jesus made arrangements in advance through an unnamed disciple.
11: kai ereit ho oikodespotē tēs oikias — and say to the owner of the house — oikodespotēs (owner/master of the house — from oikos + despotēs = house-master; also used in the parables at Luke 12:39; Luke 13:25; Luke 14:21; Luke 22:11). Ho didaskalos legei soi, pou estin to katalyma — the Teacher says to you, where is the guest room — didaskalos (Teacher) is the title the disciples use for Jesus in formal address (Luke 7:40; Luke 9:38; Luke 10:25; Luke 11:45; Luke 12:13; Luke 18:18; Luke 19:39; Luke 21:7; Luke 22:11). Katalyma (guest room — from katalyō = loose/unyoke, the stopping place where animals are unharnessed; also used at 2:7 for the "inn" where there was no room).
12: anōgeon mega estrōmenon — a large upper room furnished — anōgeon (upper room — from anō = above + gē = earth; an upper-story room; only here and Mark 14:15 in the NT; possibly the same room as the hyperōon = upper room of Acts 1:13 where the disciples were gathered). Mega (large) + estrōmenon (furnished, spread out — perfect passive participle of strōnnymi = spread, furnish; the room is already equipped with couches and table-covering). The room is ready; the disciples need only supply the Passover elements.
13: apelthontes de heuron kathōs eirēken autois — going away, they found things just as he had told them — the fulfillment formula (kathōs eirēken = just as he had said) is Luke's pattern for confirming Jesus's prophetic foreknowledge (cf. 19:32 for the colt; 24:24 for the women's report). The disciples' finding confirms that the prediction was accurate in every detail; the narrative invites the reader to trust Jesus's subsequent predictions (Peter's denial, the passion, the resurrection) with the same confidence. Kai hētoimasan to pascha — and they prepared the Passover — the preparation was completed without incident.
14: kai hote egeneto hē hōra — and when the hour came — hōra (hour) in the Johannine theological sense appears in Luke at the passion narrative: the "hour" (hōra) in ancient timekeeping is the appointed moment, but theologically it carries the weight of the divinely determined moment of crisis (cf. John 2:4; Luke 7:30; Luke 8:20; Luke 12:23, 27; Luke 13:1; Luke 17:1). Anepesen kai hoi apostoloi syn autō — he reclined and the apostles with him — anapiptō (recline at table — the formal dining posture); Luke uses apostoloi (apostles — the commissioned ones, from apostellō = send) rather than "disciples" for the Twelve at this meal, emphasizing their official status.
15: epithymia epethymēsa touto to pascha phagein meth hymōn pro tou me pathein — with desire I have desired to eat this Passover with you before I suffer — the construction epithymia epethymēsa (desire I have desired — a figure of speech known as figura etymologica: noun + cognate verb for emphasis) is a Semitic intensification idiom (cf. Genesis 2:17 LXX: thanatō apothaneisthe = you will surely die; also Luke 8:5 LXX). Epithymia (desire, longing — also used for the prodigal's longing, Luke 15:16; Lazarus's longing, Luke 16:21) with the force of "I have earnestly/urgently desired." Pro tou me pathein (before I suffer — paschō = suffer; the passion-verb) frames this Passover as the final one before the passion event.
16: ou mē phagō auto heōs hotou plērōthē en tē basileia tou theou — I will not eat it again until it finds fulfillment in the kingdom of God — the strong negation ou mē (definitely not, certainly not) + aorist subjunctive phagō (eat) is the Greek emphatic negative. Plērōthē (be fulfilled — aorist passive subjunctive of plēroō; fulfilled/completed) + en tē basileia tou theou (in the kingdom of God): the Passover meal will have its ultimate fulfillment in the eschatological kingdom-feast. The implication is that Jesus will next eat the Passover in its fulfilled form — the messianic banquet (Isaiah 25:6; Luke 13:29; Luke 14:15-24; Revelation 19:9).
17: dexamenos potērion eucharistēsas eipen — taking a cup, giving thanks he said — the cup-with-thanksgiving before the bread is unusual; if this is the order recorded by Luke, it may be the cup of the Passover Seder (the first or second cup) before the eucharistic cup (Luke 22:20), or Luke may present a compressed version. Eucharistēsas (having given thanks — aorist participle of eucharistō = give thanks; from eu- + charis = well-grace; the origin of "Eucharist"). Labete touto kai diameristate eis heautous — take this and divide it among yourselves — the cup is shared among all the disciples; the sharing is communal.
18: legō gar hymin, ou mē piō apo tou nyn apo tou genēmatos tēs ampelou — for I say to you, I will certainly not drink from now on from the fruit of the vine — the strong negation ou mē piō (I will definitely not drink) + apo tou nyn (from now on — Luke's distinctive temporal phrase; also Luke 1:48; Luke 5:10; Luke 12:52; Luke 22:18, 69; Acts 18:6). Genēmatos tēs ampelou (fruit of the vine — genēma = produce, product; ampelos = vine; the rabbinic Passover formula for wine: peri gennēmatos hagapelou) is the Passover Seder's formulaic reference to wine. Heōs hou hē basileia tou theou elthē — until the kingdom of God comes — the eschatological horizon governs all Jesus's final-meal statements.
19: labōn arton eucharistēsas eklasen kai edōken autois legōn — taking bread, giving thanks, he broke it and gave it to them saying — the fourfold eucharistic action: labōn (taking), eucharistēsas (giving thanks), eklasen (broke — aorist of klaō; the breaking is the distribution-preparation), edōken (gave). The fourfold sequence is identical at Luke 9:16 (feeding of five thousand) and Luke 24:30 (Emmaus meal); Luke frames every meal of Jesus in this pattern. touto estin to sōma mou to hyper hymōn didomenon — this is my body given for you — to sōma mou (my body — sōma = body, the whole person in bodily existence); eis tēn emēn anamnēsin — in remembrance of me — anamnēsis (remembrance, memorial re-presentation — from ana- + mimnēskō = remember again; in the LXX cultic usage, anamnēsis is the memorial sacrifice that re-presents before God the original redemptive event; Exodus 12:14; Leviticus 24:7; Numbers 10:10; not mere mental recollection but a liturgical re-enactment that participates in the original event).
20: to potērion hōsautōs meta to deipnēsai, legōn, touto to potērion hē kainē diathēkē en tō aimati mou — the cup likewise after supper, saying: this cup is the new covenant in my blood — Textual note: This verse is absent from some Western manuscripts (notably Codex Bezae/D), leading to debate between the "shorter text" (Luke 22:17-18 only, without a second cup) and the "longer text" (Luke 22:17-20, with two cups). The majority of manuscripts and the Nestle-Aland text include Luke 22:19b-20. Kainē diathēkē (new covenant — kainē = new in quality/character, not merely new in time; diathēkē = covenant, but also the Greek word for "testament/will/disposition"; the LXX uses diathēkē for Hebrew berît = covenant) — Jeremiah 31:31-34 is the background for the new covenant promise; Jesus identifies his blood as the covenant-ratifying sacrifice (Exodus 24:8: "the blood of the covenant"). Ekchunnomenon hyper hymōn (poured out for you — present participle indicating the self-giving is already in motion).
21: plēn idou hē cheir tou paradidrontos me met emou epi tēs trapezēs — but behold, the hand of the one betraying me is with mine on the table — plēn (but, however, nevertheless — Luke's adversative conjunction; a term of contrast) + idou (behold — the attention-focusing particle). Ho paradidous me (the one betraying me — present participle of paradidōmi = hand over, betray; the passion-verb that runs through all four Gospels for the betrayal). The hand (cheir) on the table during the meal is the intimacy betrayal: table fellowship creates loyalty obligations in Mediterranean culture; the betrayer violates the most sacred form of social trust.
22: hoti ho huios tou anthrōpou kata to hōrismenon poreuetai — for the Son of Man goes as it has been determined — hōrismenon (determined, decreed — perfect passive participle of horizō = set a boundary, determine, designate; from horos = boundary; the word from which "horizon"; also Acts 2:23: tē hōrismenē boulē = by the determined will; Acts 10:42; Luke 17:26, 31). The divine determination is the theological frame for the passion: what is happening is not an accident or a triumph of evil but the execution of the divine plan. plēn ouai tō anthrōpō ekeinō di hou paradidotai — but woe to that man through whom he is betrayed — moral responsibility coexists with divine determination; the betrayer is not excused by the divine plan.
23: kai autoi ērxanto suzētein pros heautous to tis ara eiē ex autōn ho touto mellōn prassein — they began to question among themselves which of them it might be who would do this — suzēteō (investigate, question among themselves — from sun- + zēteō; to search/investigate together). The optative tis ara eiē (who might it be — present optative in indirect question) reflects the uncertainty and deliberative mood. Ara (then, indeed, in that case — a particle of inference); the question is not rhetorical but genuinely investigative: the disciples do not know who the betrayer is. Luke's placement of the betrayal announcement at the table (unlike Mark/Matthew where it precedes the words of institution) creates greater dramatic tension.
24: egeneto de kai philoneikia en autois — a dispute also arose among them — philoneikia (love of strife, quarreling spirit, competitive disputing — from phileō = love + neikos = strife/quarrel; the love-of-quarreling attitude; hapax legomenon in the NT, occurring only here) is Luke's unique word for the greatness-dispute that Matthew and Mark place earlier (Matthew 18:1; Mark 9:34). Luke's placement of this dispute at the Last Supper — immediately following the words of institution — is the sharpest possible irony: the disciples quarrel about greatness at the moment Jesus institutes the memorial of his self-giving death.
25: hoi basileis tōn ethnōn kyrieuousin autōn kai hoi exousiazōntes autōn euergetai kalountai — the kings of the Gentiles lord it over them and those who exercise authority are called Benefactors — kyrieuō (lord it over, dominate — from kyrios = lord; to exercise lordship over; used negatively for domination, positively for Christ's lordship in Romans 6:9; Luke 14:9) + exousiazō (exercise authority — from exousia = authority). Euergetai (Benefactors — from eu- + ergon = well-doer; the Hellenistic royal title Euergetes was claimed by Ptolemy III, Ptolemy VIII, Antiochus VII, and others; rulers who extracted wealth from subjects while calling themselves benefactors; Jesus names the hypocrisy of the title).
26: hymeis de ouch houtōs — but you are not to be like that — the adversative de + ouch houtōs (not in this way) establishes the kingdom alternative: inversion of the Gentile power model. All ho meizōn en hymin ginesthō hōs ho neōteros — instead, the greatest among you must become like the youngest — ho neōteros (the younger/newest — comparative of neos; in Mediterranean society the youngest had the lowest status and served the older); ho hēgoumenos (the one who leads — the leadership word, from hēgeomai = lead, consider; also used for community leaders in Hebrews 13:7, 17, 24) contrasted with ho diakonōn (the one who serves — the servant/deacon word).
27: tis gar meizōn, ho anakeimenos ē ho diakonōn — for who is greater, the one who reclines or the one who serves — the rhetorical question assumes the obvious answer: the table-guest who reclines (anakeimenos = reclining at table, the honored dining position) is greater than the servant (diakonōn = one who serves/ministers). egō de en mesō hymōn eimi hōs ho diakonōn — but I am among you as the one who serves — Jesus places himself in the category of the servant (ho diakonōn), not the master. This saying interprets the footwashing (John 13) that Luke does not record; the verbal claim and the physical act are two expressions of the same truth. The diakoneō word-group (service/ministry) becomes the defining term for leadership in the early church (Acts 6:1-4; 1 Timothy 3:8-13).
28: hymeis de este hoi diamenēkotes met emou en tois peirasmois mou — you are the ones who have remained with me in my trials — diamenō (remain through, continue with — intensive compound of dia- + menō = remain through; persistent staying; also Galatians 2:5 for maintaining the truth) in the perfect participle diamenēkotes (have remained, have stayed alongside): the disciples' loyal presence through the trials (peirasmois = trials, testing — the same word as "temptation" in 4:13 and the Lord's Prayer; here the trials of Jesus's ministry under opposition) is acknowledged before the betrayal and denial are announced.
29: kagō diatithmai hymin kathōs dietheto moi ho patēr mou basileian — and I appoint/covenant to you, as my Father appointed to me, a kingdom — diatithēmi (appoint, covenant — from dia- + tithēmi = set/place through; the covenant-making verb; the diathēkē = covenant is the noun form; here Jesus makes a covenant-appointment with the disciples). The Father has made a covenant-appointment of a kingdom to Jesus; Jesus now makes a covenant-appointment of the same kingdom to the disciples. The chain of covenanted kingdom-authority runs: Father → Son → Twelve. Basileian (a kingdom — the object of the covenant-appointment) is what is being transmitted.
30: hina esthiēte kai pinēte epi tēs trapezēs mou en tē basileia mou kai kathēsesthe epi thronōn — so that you may eat and drink at my table in my kingdom and sit on thrones — the kingdom-appointment includes both table-fellowship (esthiēte kai pinēte = eat and drink) and judicial authority (kathēsesthe epi thronōn = sit on thrones — the same authority-symbol as Matthew 19:28). Krinontes tas dōdeka phulas tou Israēl — judging the twelve tribes of Israel — krinō (judge — the judicial authority verb); the twelve apostles' eschatological authority over the twelve tribes is both a governance and a prophetic witness role; the promise anticipates the reconstitution of Israel under the messianic king.
31: Simōn Simōn, idou ho Satanas exēitēsato hymas tou sinisai hōs ton siton — Simon, Simon, behold Satan has demanded you to sift you like wheat — the doubled name (Simōn Simōn) signals urgency and solemnity (cf. Abraham, Abraham — Genesis 22:11; Moses, Moses — Exodus 3:4; Martha, Martha — Luke 10:41; Saul, Saul — Acts 9:4). Exaiteō (demand, ask for from — from ex- + aiteō = ask out from; to request/demand from someone for a specific purpose; only here in the NT) — Satan has formally requested (from God) permission to test the disciples, as at Job 1:6-12. Siniasai hōs ton siton (sift like wheat — siniazo = to pass through a sieve; the wheat is tossed in the sieve to separate grain from chaff; the disciples are the wheat; the sifting will test their quality). Hymas (plural: all of you — the whole group is under threat).
32: egō de edeēthēn peri sou hina mē eklipē hē pistis sou — but I have prayed for you that your faith may not fail — edeēthēn (I prayed, I besought — aorist of deomai = ask, beseech; the earnest-petition verb; the singular sou [you/your] shifts from the plural of Luke 22:31 to the singular: Jesus prays specifically for Simon). Hina mē eklipē hē pistis sou — that your faith may not fail — ekleipō (fail, give out, cease — also used for the sun failing at Luke 23:45; "eclipse" is from this root). kai su pote epistrepsas stērison tous adelphous sou — and when you have turned back, strengthen your brothers — epistrepho (turn back, return — the conversion/repentance verb; also 17:4 for repeated turning; Acts 3:19; Luke 26:20; the word for conversion throughout Luke-Acts). Stērizō (strengthen, establish — the same root as the fixed chasm at Luke 16:26; here the positive use = stabilizing the community).
33: ho de eipen autō, Kyrie, meta sou hetoimos eimi kai eis phylakēn kai eis thanaton poreuesthai — he said to him, Lord, I am ready to go with you both to prison and to death — hetoimos eimi (I am ready — hetoimos = prepared, equipped for; the readiness claim is sincere but will be tested). Eis phylakēn kai eis thanaton (to prison and to death — the extreme commitment claim covers both incarceration and execution). Peter's readiness-claim is the setup for Jesus's denial-prediction; the more emphatic the claim, the sharper the irony of the triple denial.
34: ho de eipen, Legō soi, Petros, ou phōnēsei sēmeron alektōr — he said, I tell you, Peter, the rooster will not crow today — Petros (Peter — the Aramaic Kēphas/Rock name; Jesus addresses him by name, not "Simon" as in Luke 22:31) in the prediction. Heōs tris aparnēsē me eidenai — before you have denied three times that you know me — aparneomai (deny, disown — intensive compound of apo- + arneomai = deny completely away; the total-denial word; the same verb as 9:23 for "deny oneself" and the eschatological denial of Luke 12:9). Eidenai (to know — perfect infinitive of oida; "to know" as relationship-knowledge, not merely information). The triple denial structure (tris) mirrors Peter's threefold denial in Luke 22:56-60.
35: hote apesteila hymas ater ballontiou kai pēras kai hypodēmatōn, mē tinos hysterēsate — when I sent you without purse, bag, or sandals, did you lack anything — the question references the original commissioning (Luke 9:3; Luke 10:4) when Jesus sent the disciples without supplies and they were provided for through hospitality. Hystereomai (lack, fall short — from hystereō = be behind, fall short; the "shortage" verb; also 15:14 for the prodigal falling into need) in the aorist: at any time during the mission, did you lack something? The implied answer is no — the provision model worked.
36: alla nyn ho echōn ballantion aratō, homoiōs kai pēran — but now the one who has a purse, let him take it, and likewise a bag — the reversal from the mission-without-provision (Luke 22:35) to the provision-required stance is dramatic. Kai ho mē echōn, pōlēsatō to himation autou kai agorasatō machairan — and the one who does not have, let him sell his cloak and buy a sword — machaira (sword — a short sword, fighting knife, or dagger; distinct from rhomphaia = the large broadsword). The sword-instruction has been interpreted: (1) literally (Jesus permits defensive weapons); (2) figuratively (symbolic readiness for conflict); (3) ironically (provoking the "two swords are enough" response that he dismisses). Context (the Isaiah 53:12 fulfillment in Luke 22:37) suggests the instruction is about fulfilling the "with transgressors" prophecy — appearing armed makes Jesus and the disciples look like rebels.
38: hoi de eipan, Kyrie, idou machaira hōde duo — they said, Lord, see here are two swords — the disciples produce two swords, showing they already had weapons. ho de eipen autois, hikanon estin — he said to them, it is enough — hikanon (enough, sufficient — from hikanós = sufficient, able; also 7:6 for "I am not worthy"; Luke 22:38). Jesus's response hikanon estin is deliberately ambiguous: either "two swords suffice for the literal purpose" (but two swords are obviously insufficient for a military force) or "enough of this topic" (cutting off the conversation). The second reading fits better: Jesus is ending the sword-discussion, not approving a two-sword militia. The Gethsemane incident (Luke 22:51) where Jesus stops the sword-use confirms this reading.
39: kai exelthōn eporeuthē kata to ethos eis to oros tōn elaiōn — going out, he went according to his custom to the Mount of Olives — kata to ethos (according to the custom — ethos = custom, habit; Luke's note that this was habitual: Jesus regularly went to the Mount of Olives during his Jerusalem days; cf. Luke 21:37: "each day he taught in the temple and each evening went out to the Mount of Olives"). The Mount of Olives is east of Jerusalem across the Kidron Valley; the Gethsemane garden is on its western slope. Ēkolouthēsan de autō kai hoi mathētai — his disciples followed him — the disciples follow as always; they do not know yet what this particular evening holds.
40: genomenos de epi tou topou eipen autois, proseuchesthe mē eiselthein eis peirasmon — arriving at the place, he said to them: pray that you will not enter into temptation — genomenos de epi tou topou (arriving at the place — ginomai epi = come to, arrive at; the place is identified as Gethsemane in Mark 14:32 and Matthew 26:36) — Luke simply says "the place" (ton topon), suggesting a known location. Proseuchesthe mē eiselthein eis peirasmon — pray that you will not enter into temptation — the prayer-against-temptation command frames both the beginning (Luke 22:40) and the end (Luke 22:46) of the Gethsemane scene in Luke, creating an inclusio; the disciples' failure to pray explains their failure to stand in the trial.
41: kai autos apespasthē ap autōn hōsei lithou bolēn — he withdrew from them about a stone's throw — apespaō (draw away, separate — from apo- + spaō = draw/pull; to withdraw deliberately; also Acts 20:30; 21:1 for the pull of departure) + hōsei lithou bolēn (about a stone's throw — a distance measurement; bolē = throw; approximately 30-50 meters). theis ta gonata prosēucheto — kneeling, he prayed — theis ta gonata (having placed the knees = kneeling — gony = knee; the kneeling posture is Luke's unique Gethsemane detail; Mark and Matthew say "falling on his face"; kneeling is the prayer posture throughout Luke-Acts: Acts 7:60; Luke 9:40; Luke 20:36; Luke 21:5).
42: Pater, ei boulei, parenengke touto to potērion ap emou — Father, if you are willing, remove this cup from me — boulei (you are willing — present indicative of boulomai = will, desire; the will-of-God verb; Luke uses boulei rather than Matthew's dynaton estin = if it is possible; Luke's conditional is about divine willingness, Matthew's about divine possibility — subtly different theological framings). Potērion (cup — the cup of divine wrath and suffering; Psalms 75:8; Isaiah 51:17, 22; Jeremiah 25:15-17; the metaphor runs through the passion narrative). plēn mē to thelēma mou alla to son ginesthō — yet not my will, but yours be done — thelēma (will — from thelō) in the contrast: Jesus's thelēma (will, desire) vs the Father's thelēma. The prayer is the Son's full submission of his own will to the Father's will — the inverse of Adam's assertion of human will against God.
43: ōphthē de autō angelos ap ouranou enischyōn auton — an angel from heaven appeared to him, strengthening him — Luke 22:43-44 are textually uncertain; they are absent from important manuscripts (p75, Codex Alexandrinus B, Codex Vaticanus, some Syriac versions) and present in others (Codex Sinaiticus, the Byzantine tradition). The UBS/NA text brackets them as secondary. If original: ōphthē (appeared — aorist passive of horaō; the appearance verb for angelic and divine manifestations throughout Luke-Acts; Luke 1:11; Luke 9:31; Acts 2:3; Luke 7:26, 30, 35). Enischyōn (strengthening — present participle of enischuō = give strength in/through; from en- + ischuō = strengthen within; only here and Acts 9:19 in the NT). The angel's strengthening echoes the angels' service after the wilderness temptation (Matthew 4:11; Mark 1:13).
44: kai genomenos en agōnia ekteneteron prosēucheto — being in agony he prayed more earnestly — also textually disputed (see Luke 22:43). Agōnia (agony, intense struggle — from agōn = athletic contest, struggle; the word for the intense physical/mental strain of an athletic agonist; only here in the NT) describes the psychological-physical intensity of the prayer. Ekteneteron (more earnestly — comparative adverb of ektenōs = earnestly, with outstretched effort; from ekteinō = stretch out). egeneto de ho hidrōs autou hōsei thromboi haimatos katabainontes epi tēn gēn — his sweat became like drops/clots of blood falling to the ground — thromboi haimatos (drops/clots of blood — thrombos = clot, lump; in medical writers the clotted form of blood; Luke the physician's choice of the precise medical term for blood that has thickened; the sweat was not blood but resembled blood-clots in its appearance).
45: kai anastas apo tēs proseuchēs, elthōn pros tous mathētas, heuren koimōmenous autous apo tēs lypēs — rising from prayer and coming to the disciples, he found them sleeping from grief — anastas (rising — the resurrection verb in its mundane use) + apo tēs proseuchēs (from the prayer — a notable prepositional phrase: Jesus rises from the prayer encounter as from a meeting). Apo tēs lypēs (from grief — lypē = grief, sorrow; Luke's unique explanation for the disciples' sleep; Matthew says simply they were sleeping; Luke adds the cause: the disciples are not lazy but overwhelmed by sorrow). The disciples feel the weight of the approaching passion even if they do not understand it.
46: ti katheudete, anastatontes proseuchesthe, hina mē eiselthēte eis peirasmon — why are you sleeping? Rise and pray that you may not enter into temptation — the inclusio-closing command (matching Luke 22:40) frames the entire Gethsemane scene between two identical prayer-against-temptation commands. The disciples have failed to follow the instruction of Luke 22:40; their failure to pray will result in their failure to stand when tested: Peter will deny (Luke 22:54-62) and the others will scatter (implied). The prayer against temptation (hina mē eiselthēte eis peirasmon) is the practical means of perseverance that the disciples have neglected.
47: eti autou lalountos idou ochlos — while he was still speaking, behold a crowd — eti autou lalountos (while he was still speaking — genitive absolute construction: the ongoing speech is interrupted) + idou (behold — the attention-particle signaling a sudden arrival). Ho legomenos Ioudas heis tōn dōdeka proeporeueto autōn — the one called Judas, one of the Twelve, was leading them — proporeuomai (lead ahead of, go before — compound of pro- + poreuomai; Judas is at the front of the arresting party; he knows the location from his own time with Jesus there). Ēngisen tō Iēsou philēsai auton — he drew near to Jesus to kiss him — philesai (to kiss — infinitive of phileō = love, kiss; the friendship-kiss of greeting, not agapaō).
48: Ioudas, philēmati ton huion tou anthrōpou paradidōs — Judas, are you betraying the Son of Man with a kiss — the rhetorical question rather than a command; Jesus identifies Judas's action without stopping it. Philēmati (with a kiss — the instrumental dative; the instrument of betrayal is the greeting that signals friendship and affection). The contrast between philēma (kiss of affection) and paradidōmi (betray, hand over) is the moral grotesque of the scene. Jesus names Judas by name and identifies the action, perhaps giving him one final moment to reconsider, but the arrest proceeds.
49: idontes de hoi peri auton to esomenon eipan — seeing what was going to happen, those around him said — hoi peri auton (those around him — the disciples as a group; the "around him" phrase for close associates). Kyrie, ei pataxomen en machairē — Lord, should we strike with a sword — patassō (strike, hit — the blow-word; also used for the angel striking Herod, Acts 12:23) + en machairē (with a sword — the short sword from Luke 22:36-38). The disciples ask permission before acting; the question implies they are not sure whether to use the weapons. Luke's version makes the disciples' action a question; the blow (Luke 22:50) happens immediately after, perhaps without waiting for an answer.
50: kai epataxen heis tis ex autōn tou archierēos ton doulon kai apheilen to ous autou to dexion — one of them struck the servant of the high priest and cut off his right ear — heis tis ex autōn (one of them — unnamed in Luke; John 18:10 names him as Peter) + ton doulon (the servant — doulos = slave, servant; John 18:10 names him Malchus) + to ous to dexion (the right ear — the specificity of "right" is preserved across all four Gospels). The detail of the right ear is consistent across the tradition; in ancient combat, reaching the opponent's right ear requires swinging a right-handed stroke that the opponent has partially blocked (reducing it from a lethal head blow to an ear).
51: apokritheis de ho Iēsous eipen, Eate heōs toutou — Jesus replied: Stop, no more of this — eate heōs toutou (let be as far as this — eaō = let, allow; heōs toutou = as far as this/up to this point; the command to stop). kai hapsamenos tou ōtiou iāsato auton — and touching the ear, he healed him — hapsamenos (touching — aorist participle of haptō = touch; the healing-touch verb throughout Luke: Luke 5:13; Luke 6:19; Luke 7:14; Luke 8:44-47). Iāsato (healed — aorist of iaomai = heal thoroughly) + auton (him = the high priest's servant). This detail is unique to Luke; only Luke records that Jesus healed the wounded ear of his own arrestor at the moment of arrest — the final miracle of Jesus's ministry is an act of mercy on an enemy.
52: eipen de Iēsous pros tous paragenomenous ep auton archiereis kai stratēgous tou hierou kai presbyterous — Jesus said to the chief priests, officers of the temple, and elders who had come for him — the three-element arrest party (chief priests + temple officers + elders) represents the full Sanhedrin structure: the priestly aristocracy, the temple police, and the lay elders. Hōs epi lēstēn exēlthate meta machairōn kai xylōn — as against a robber you came out with swords and clubs — lēstēs (robber, brigand — from lēizomai = plunder; the term for an armed criminal; also Barabbas is a lēstēs at Luke 23:19; the same word Jesus used in 10:30 and Luke 19:46). The comparison to a robbery-arrest is an ironic protest: Jesus has been teaching openly in the temple daily; the nighttime armed arrest treats him as a dangerous criminal.
53: kath hēmeran ontos mou meth hymōn en tō hierō ouk exeteinate tas cheiras ep eme — every day I was with you in the temple and you did not stretch out your hands against me — kath hēmeran (daily, every day — Luke's characteristic temporal phrase, also Luke 9:23; Luke 11:3; Luke 16:19; Luke 19:47) + ontos mou meth hymōn (while I was with you — genitive absolute) + en tō hierō (in the temple — the public space where arrest was impossible due to the crowd). all autē estin hymōn hē hōra kai hē exousia tou skotous — but this is your hour and the power of darkness — hē exousia tou skotous (the authority of darkness — exousia = authority, power; skotos = darkness; the "authority of darkness" is a uniquely Lukan phrase, naming the cosmic dimension of the arrest; also Colossians 1:13 which contrasts it with the kingdom of the Son).
54: syllabanōtes de auton ēgagon kai eisēgagon eis tēn oikian tou archierōs — seizing him, they led him away and brought him into the house of the high priest — syllambanō (seize, arrest — the arrest verb; also Luke 1:24, 31, 36 for the supernatural conceptions; here the physical arrest) + eisagō (bring into — the compound entry verb). The high priest's house (oikia tou archiereōs) is the location for the preliminary examination before formal Sanhedrin proceedings. Ho de Petros ēkolouthei makrothen — Peter followed at a distance — makrothen (from a distance — the spatial adverb of safe following distance); the courageous follower who swore to go to prison and death (Luke 22:33) now follows at a safe remove.
55: periaapsantōn de pyr en mesō tēs aulēs kai synkathisantōn hēlthen ho Petros eis to meson autōn — kindling a fire in the middle of the courtyard and sitting down together, Peter sat down in the middle of them — periapsantōn pyr (kindling a fire — periaptō = kindle around, light around; from peri- + haptō; a fire lit in the center of the courtyard for warmth on a cold spring night). Peter integrates himself into the group around the fire (eis to meson autōn = into the middle of them): the strategy of camouflage, of blending in with the crowd near the fire that will lead to his identification and denial.
56: idousa de auton paidiskē tis kathēmenon pros to phōs kai atenisasa autō eipen — a servant girl saw him sitting at the firelight and staring at him she said — paidiskē (servant girl — diminutive of pais = child, servant; a young female slave) + atenisasa (staring, gazing intently — aorist participle of atenizō = gaze fixedly; from atenēs = stretched, strained; Luke's characteristic intensive-gaze verb: Luke 4:20; Acts 1:10; Luke 3:4, 12; Luke 6:15; Luke 7:55; Luke 10:4; Luke 11:6; Luke 13:9; Luke 14:9; Luke 23:1). The firelight (pros to phōs) is what enables the servant girl to see Peter's face clearly — the light that betrays him.
57: ho de ērnēsato auton legōn, Ouk oida auton, gynai — he denied him, saying, Woman, I do not know him — arneomai (deny, disown — the simple form; the intensive aparneomai was used in Jesus's prediction at Luke 22:34; here the simpler form but the same act). Ouk oida auton (I do not know him — oida = know, be acquainted with; relationship-knowledge, not mere information) directly contradicts the relational knowledge that discipleship is. The address gynai (woman — the formal/neutral address, not rude but also not warm; cf. John 2:4; Luke 4:21; Luke 19:26; Luke 20:13) to the servant girl is the first step of Peter's withdrawal from relationship.
58: kai meta brachy heteros idōn auton ephē, kai sy ex autōn ei — a little later another one saw him and said, you also are one of them — meta brachy (a little later — brachus = short; the brief interval allows Peter to think the danger has passed). Kai sy ex autōn ei (you also are one of them — the "also" = kai implicates Peter in the group of Jesus's followers). Ho de Petros ephē, Anthrōpe, ouk eimi — Peter said, Man, I am not — anthrōpe (man — the address to a male addressee, neutral but slightly defensive); the denial is flat and simple: ouk eimi (I am not) — the direct contradiction of the truth, and an ironic contrast to Jesus's "I am" (egō eimi) before the Sanhedrin (Luke 22:70).
59: kai diastasēs hōsei hōras mias allos tis diischurizeto legōn, Ep alētheias kai houtos met autou ēn — about an hour later someone else insisted emphatically, truly this man also was with him — diastasēs hōras mias (about an hour having elapsed — diistēmi = stand apart, elapse; the time-interval verb). Diischyrizomai (insist, maintain strongly — from dia- + ischyrizō = strengthen through; to insist with force; also Acts 12:15) — the third accuser is emphatic, not tentative. Ep alētheias (truly, in truth — the truth-assertion formula) + Galillaios gar estin (for he is a Galilean — the dialect identification; Galilean Greek had a distinctive accent that identified speakers to Judeans).
60: eipen ho Petros, Anthrōpe, ouk oida ho legeis — Peter said, Man, I do not know what you are saying — the third denial escalates to total ignorance: not just "I don't know him" (Luke 22:57) or "I am not one of them" (Luke 22:58), but "I don't even understand what you're talking about." Kai parachrēma eti lalountos autou ephōnēsen ho alektōr — and immediately while he was still speaking the rooster crowed — parachrēma (immediately — Luke's characteristic instantaneous-fulfillment adverb; also Luke 1:64; Luke 4:39; Luke 5:25; Luke 8:44, 47; Luke 13:13; Luke 18:43; Luke 19:11; Acts 3:7; Luke 5:10; Luke 12:23; Luke 13:11; Luke 16:26, 33). The rooster's crow (ephōnēsen ho alektōr) arrives at the moment of the third denial, fulfilling Jesus's prediction exactly.
61: kai strapheis ho kyrios eneblepsēn tō Petrō — and the Lord turned and looked at Peter — strapheis ho kyrios (the Lord turned — aorist passive of strephō; "the Lord" is Luke's post-resurrection title used here proleptically and throughout the passion narrative) is unique to Luke: no other Gospel records Jesus turning to look at Peter during the denial. Eneblepsen (looked at, gazed at — aorist of emblepō = look into/at; to look directly, intently at someone). The wordless gaze of the Lord on Peter at the moment of the crowing rooster is the narrative heart of the denial account: no rebuke, no words — only the silent look that recalls the prediction and breaks Peter's heart.
62: kai exelthōn exō eklausen pikrōs — going outside he wept bitterly — exelthōn exō (going outside — the redundant directional compounds: going-out outside, the emphatic exit from the scene of his shame). Eklausen (wept — aorist of klaiō = weep, cry; not just tears in the eyes but active weeping) + pikrōs (bitterly — from pikros = bitter; the sharp, cutting quality of bitter weeping, grief that burns). Peter's bitter weeping is the authentic response to the recognition of his failure; it is the beginning of the turning (epistrepsas) that Jesus predicted in Luke 22:32.
63: kai hoi andres hoi synechontes auton enepaizon autō dernontes — the men who held him began mocking and beating him — synechontes (holding, guarding — from sunechō = hold together, restrain; the temple-guard holding him in custody) + empaizoō (mock, ridicule — from en- + paizō = play at someone's expense; the mockery verb; also Luke 14:29; Luke 18:32; Luke 23:11, 36) + derontes (beating — from derō = flay, beat; used in Luke 12:47-48 for the servant beaten with blows; a physical striking). Luke's night-session mockery by the guards contrasts with the morning session before the Sanhedrin (Luke 22:66-71); the mockery is by the soldiers while the official proceedings are by the council.
64: kai perikalypsantes auton epērōtōn legontes, Prophēteuson, tis estin ho paisas se — blindfolding him they questioned him saying, Prophesy! Who hit you — perikalyptō (blindfold, cover around — from peri- + kalyptō = cover completely around; to blindfold by covering the face). The mockery-game is ironic: they ask the Prophet to prophesy who struck him while blindfolded. The irony is that he has already prophesied accurately (Peter's denial, Judas's betrayal, the passion) and will prophesy accurately (the temple's destruction, the Son of Man's coming). The blindfold hides his accusers from him while revealing their mockery to Luke's readers.
65: kai hetera polla blasphēmountes elegon eis auton — and many other things insulting him they said against him — blasphēmeō (insult, revile, blaspheme — from blaptō = harm + phēmē = word/speech; to harm with words, to speak injuriously; in NT usage, blasphemy against God is the most serious form but the word covers human insult as well). The summary hetera polla (many other things) compresses the full extent of the abuse into an open-ended notation; Luke does not specify every insult but signals that the mockery was extensive and verbal as well as physical.
66: kai hōs egeneto hēmera, sunēchthē to presbyterion tou laou, archiereis te kai grammateis — and when day came, the council of the elders of the people was assembled, both chief priests and scribes — hōs egeneto hēmera (when day came — Luke's daybreak marker; the night's beatings give way to the formal morning session) + presbyterion (council of elders — the Senate of the elders; presbyterion = body of elders, the presbutery; also Acts 22:5; 1 Timothy 4:14 for the council of elders that laid hands on Timothy). Synēgagon auton eis to synedrion autōn — they led him into their council — synedrion (Sanhedrin — from syn- + edra = seat together; the council of 70 elders + high priest; the supreme Jewish judicial body).
67: ei su ei ho Christos, eipon hēmin — if you are the Christ, tell us — the Sanhedrin's question places the messianic claim as the primary issue. Iēsous de eipen autois, Ean hymin eipō, ou mē pisteusēte — Jesus said to them: If I tell you, you will not believe — ou mē pisteusēte (you will certainly not believe — double negative with aorist subjunctive; the emphatic Greek negative). Jesus's response identifies the questioners' disposition as already determined: they are not inquiring to believe but to find accusation material. The conditional (ean = if) + ou mē (certainly not) = the impossibility of genuine response from this audience.
68: ean de erōtēsō, ou mē apokrithēte — and if I ask you, you will not answer — the second conditional extends the no-answer dynamic in both directions: Jesus cannot get a genuine response by telling them directly, and cannot elicit genuine engagement by asking them. The symmetry of non-response (they won't believe; they won't answer) identifies the trial as performative rather than genuine inquiry; the verdict is predetermined. The statement makes the formal appearance of a trial even more hollow.
69: apo tou nyn de estai ho huios tou anthrōpou kathēmenos ek dexiōn tēs dynameōs tou theou — from now on the Son of Man will be seated at the right hand of the power of God — apo tou nyn (from now on — Luke's distinctive temporal phrase: Luke 1:48; Luke 5:10; Luke 12:52; Luke 22:18, 69; Acts 18:6; only here at the trial among the Synoptics; Matthew and Mark lack this phrase) — the temporal marker apo tou nyn indicates that the enthronement begins from the moment of the passion/exaltation, not only at the Parousia. Kathēmenos ek dexiōn (seated at the right hand — Psalms 110:1 allusion; the position of co-regent authority) + tēs dynameōs tou theou (the power of God — dynamis = power; "Power" as a divine circumlocution; "seated at the right hand of Power/God").
70: eipan de pantes, Su oun ei ho huios tou theou — they all said: Are you then the Son of God — the council draws the implication from Jesus's Son-of-Man-at-God's-right-hand claim: if you will sit at God's right hand, you are claiming divine Sonship. The oun (then, therefore) is inferential: the Son of Man claim implies the Son of God claim. ho de pros autous ephē, Hymeis legete hoti egō eimi — he said to them: You say that I am — hymeis legete (you say — the second-person emphasis) + hoti egō eimi (that I am — the egō eimi = "I am" formula, though here embedded in a reported speech clause; the absolute egō eimi resonates with the divine self-identification of Exodus 3:14). The response neither affirms nor denies but lets their formulation stand as their own conclusion.
71: hoi de eipan, Ti eti echomen martyrias chreian? autoi gar ēkousamen apo tou stomatos autou — they said: What further need do we have of testimony? For we ourselves have heard from his own mouth — martyrias chreian (need of testimony — martyria = testimony, witness; chreia = need, necessity; they declare the testimony phase complete) + autoi gar ēkousamen (for we ourselves heard — the emphatic autoi = we ourselves, with no further witnesses needed). Apo tou stomatos autou (from his own mouth — the oral self-testimony that in Jewish law required no corroboration). The Sanhedrin's conclusion closes the chapter with their declaration that the trial is complete and a conviction is reached on Jesus's own words; Luke's readers know the irony that the truth spoken as self-condemnation is the very truth that justifies the condemned.
